principles: Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
Avoid SELECT * in production queries; specify only needed columns
Use appropriate JOIN types (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT) based on data requirements
Limit result sets when possible (LIMIT clause)
Use EXISTS instead of IN for subqueries when checking existence
Avoid correlated subqueries when a JOIN would be more efficient
Use UNION ALL instead of UNION when duplicates don't need to be removed
Consider using window functions for complex aggregations
Use CTEs (Common Table Expressions) for complex queries to improve readability
Optimize ORDER BY by ensuring indexed columns are used
performanceTips: Filter early in the query (WHERE before JOINs when possible)
Use appropriate data types to reduce storage and improve performance
Avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE clauses (prevents index usage)
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to identify slow query parts
Consider materialized views for expensive aggregations
Use connection pooling to reduce connection overhead
commonMistakes: Using LIKE '%pattern%' (prevents index usage; use LIKE 'pattern%' or full-text search)
Comparing different data types (causes implicit casting and prevents index usage)
Using OR conditions that prevent index usage (consider UNION)
N+1 query problems (fetch related data in one query with JOINs)
Not using prepared statements for repeated queries
